behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive error messages.
behaviors.justification: Cron parsing is pure string validation with no side effects. The parser applies deterministic rules (month ∈ [0-11], day ∈ [1-31], syntax compliance) to reject invalid input. A unit test directly invoking parse_cron(invalid_expr) exhaustively tests all validation paths. Real failures (accepting invalid input, unclear errors) are caught immediately. No mocking, I/O, or external dependencies involved.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Call parse_cron() with invalid expressions covering all error cases (month 13, day 32, incomplete fields, malformed syntax). Verify that the correct exception type is raised and error message is descriptive for each invalid pattern.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like 'last day of month' and 'nearest weekday.'
behaviors.justification: Next-run computation is deterministic arithmetic: given a cron schedule and start time, calculate the next N occurrences. The algorithm is self-contained with no external dependencies. Real failures (miscalculation for edge cases like Feb 29, nearest weekday spanning weekends) are caught by unit tests with fixed inputs and assertion-based outputs. No database, subprocess, or task executor required.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Call compute_next_runs(schedule, start_datetime, count) with parametrized cron expressions covering standard cases (daily, monthly, yearly) and edge cases (last day of month in leap/non-leap years, nearest weekday, leap-day arithmetic). Assert returned timestamps exactly match expected values.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: Overlap detection is pure set-intersection logic: compare timestamps from two schedules against a minimum-gap threshold. The algorithm is deterministic, acyclic, and self-contained. Real failures (false negatives missing overlaps, false positives on non-overlapping schedules) are caught by unit tests that feed known overlapping/non-overlapping pairs and assert correct detection. No I/O, mocking, or subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Call detect_overlaps(schedule_a, schedule_b, min_gap_minutes) with parametrized schedule pairs: overlapping pairs (e.g., 9:00 AM and 9:30 AM with 30-min gap) must be detected; non-overlapping pairs (e.g., 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM with 30-min gap) must not. Verify conflict windows are reported with correct start/end times.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition — a job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly.
behaviors.justification: DST handling is deterministic computation using real tzinfo objects (from system tzdata via zoneinfo/pytz). Spring-forward (2:00–2:59 AM doesn't exist) and fall-back (1:00–1:59 AM occurs twice) are handled deterministically by the timezone library. Unit tests using real tzinfo objects and fixed dates compute next-run timestamps across transitions and assert correctness. The tzdata is static for a given date, so results are reproducible in-process. No subprocess, network, or system-level variability required.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Call compute_next_runs(schedule, start_datetime, timezone, count) with real tzinfo objects (zoneinfo.ZoneInfo) for US/Eastern. Test job scheduled at 2:30 AM on 2024-03-10 (spring-forward gap) and 1:30 AM on 2024-11-03 (fall-back ambiguity). Assert computed timestamps are correct per DST-aware policy (skipped, shifted, or both occurrences handled correctly).

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's computed next run time has already passed, the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: Missed-run decision logic is a pure conditional tree: if next_run_time < current_time AND policy allows → backfill; else skip. Deterministic, no I/O or side effects in the decision itself. Real failures (wrong decision, e.g., backfilling when policy says skip) are caught by unit tests with fixed current_time and policy settings. The test verifies the decision logic; actual job execution (if backfill triggers it) is a separate behavior and tested elsewhere.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Call determine_missed_run_action(job, current_time, policy) with parametrized combinations: past next_run_time with policy='skip' must return 'skip'; with policy='backfill' within threshold must return 'backfill'; beyond threshold must return 'skip'. Assert decisions match expected values for all threshold boundary conditions.
